← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

South Asia

Stupas, cave temples, bronze gods, and marble mausoleums.

Hindu, Buddhist, Jain, and Islamic traditions layered on top of each other for two millennia. Much of the greatest work is architecture carved straight out of living rock.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Mohenjo-daro
Indus Valley Civilization · c. 2500 BCE · Pakistan
A Bronze Age city with grid streets, covered drains, and a Great Bath. Its script is still undeciphered.
Harappa
Indus Valley Civilization · c. 2600–1900 BCE · Pakistan
One of the two great Indus cities, grid-planned like Mohenjo-daro. Its granary, citadel, and burial mounds lie under centuries of British railway ballast quarried from the ruins.
Dancing Girl of Mohenjo-daro
Indus Valley · c. 2500 BCE
A small bronze, four and a half inches tall, a slouched teenage girl with bangles up one arm. The oldest lost-wax bronze from the subcontinent — and still the confident, nameless star of the Indus.
Pashupati Seal
Indus Valley · c. 2350–2000 BCE
A steatite seal of a seated, horned figure surrounded by animals — sometimes read as a proto-Shiva, sometimes a shaman. We can't read the script that runs along the top.
Priest-King of Mohenjo-daro
Indus Valley · c. 2000 BCE · Pakistan
A small bearded steatite bust, head-band and trefoil-patterned robe. Its title is a guess: we don't know if Mohenjo-daro had priests, kings, or this man at all.
Anthropomorph
India, Bronze Age · c. 1500–1000 BCE · copper

1000 BCE – 0

Punch-Marked Coin
India, Rajasthan, Maurya period (322–185 BCE) · 400–300 BCE · silver
Ringstone
India, Maurya period (322–185 BCE) · 200s BCE · steatite
Male Head, possibly a Yaksha
Northeastern India, Bihar, Patna, Bulandibagh · 300s BCE · terracotta
Libation Cup
India, Maurya period (322–185 BCE)–Shunga period (185–78 BCE) · c. 235–185 BCE · bronze, lost wax technique
Great Stupa at Sanchi
Mauryan → Shunga · 3rd c. BCE onward
A hemispherical mound containing relics of the Buddha. The four carved gateways around it narrate his life without ever depicting him directly.
Lion Capital of Ashoka
Mauryan · c. 250 BCE · Sarnath
Four back-to-back lions atop a polished sandstone column, once topped by a wheel. Adopted as the national emblem of India in 1950.
Rampurva Bull Capital
Mauryan · 3rd c. BCE · Bihar
An Ashokan pillar capital: a single polished Chunar sandstone bull, so highly burnished it was mistaken for metal. One of the finest survivals of the Mauryan court style.
Didarganj Yakshi
Mauryan · c. 3rd c. BCE · Bihar
A monumental polished sandstone female figure with a fly-whisk. Found buried by the Ganges; her glossy Mauryan polish and voluptuous poise are thought to anticipate centuries of Indian sculpture.
Crouching Dwarf Musician
India, Shunga period (185–78 BCE) · 200–100 BCE · terracotta
Double-Faced Crossbar from a Railing
India, Madhya Pradesh, Bharhut, Shunga period · c. 150 BCE · plum-colored sandstone
Relic casket
Sanchi · ca. 200-100 BC
Double-Faced Crossbar from a Railing: Male Worshipper (recto)
India, Madhya Pradesh, Bharhut, Shunga period · c. 150 BCE · plum-colored sandstone

Section of a Coping Rail

India, Madhya Pradesh, Bharhut, Shunga period · c. 150 BCE · plum-colored sandstone
Necklace Bead in the Form of a Fish
India, Shunga period (185–78 BCE) · 185–72 BCE · gold repoussé with granulation
Necklace Beads in the Form of Fish
India, Shunga period (185–78 BCE) · 185–72 BCE · gold repoussé with granulation
Pair of Pendants in the Shape of an Auspicious Symbol
India, Shunga period (185–78 BCE) · 185–72 BCE · gold
Pendant in the Shape of a Fish-Tailed Elephant
India, Shunga period (185–78 BCE) · 185–72 BCE · gold
Ajanta Caves
Buddhist · 2nd c. BCE – 6th c. CE · Maharashtra
Thirty rock-cut monasteries with surviving murals of the Buddha's past lives. Abandoned, forgotten, and rediscovered by a tiger-hunting British officer in 1819.
Bhaja Caves
Early Buddhist · 2nd c. BCE · Maharashtra
Twenty-two rock-cut viharas and a chaitya, with wooden ribs imitated in stone. Reliefs show figures riding elephants, dancing — a rare look at secular imagery in early Buddhist art.
Bharhut Stupa Reliefs
Early Buddhist · 2nd c. BCE · India
Carved sandstone panels from the railing of a lost stupa — jataka tales, yakshas, royal processions. Before figural Buddhas existed, the Buddha appears as a footprint, a throne, or an umbrella.
Head of a Yaksha
Northern India, Uttar Pradesh, Mathura, Shunga period (185–78 BCE) · c. 125 BCE · mottled red sandstone
Ruwanwelisaya
Anuradhapura · 2nd c. BCE · Sri Lanka
A vast white-domed stupa built by King Dutugamunu — once 103 meters, restored again and again over two millennia. The bell-shape that became the standard template for Sinhalese dagobas.
Roundel with Mermaids and Lotus
India, Bengal, Chandraketugarh area, Shunga period (185–78 BCE) · c. 100 BCE · ivory
Dambulla Cave Temple
Sri Lankan Buddhist · 1st c. BCE onward
Five caves hollowed under a granite overhang, packed with over 150 Buddha figures and ceilings painted with repeating Buddha rows. A rock-cut monastery continuously used for 2,000 years.
Amorous Couple (Mithuna) with Parrot
by India
West Bengal · South Asian · -100–-1 · Molded terracotta with red slip
Toy Cart with Grimacing Potbellied Dwarf (Yaksha)

India, Shunga period (185–78 BCE) · c. 75 BCE · terracotta
Worshippers at a sacred tree
Southern India, Andhra Pradesh, possibly Chandavaram · about 50 BCE to 50 CE · limestone
Palette with Sea Nymph (Nereid) Riding on a Sea Monster
by Pakistan
Gandhara · South Asian · -99–100 · Gray chloritic schist
Karla Caves
Early Buddhist · 1st c. BCE · Maharashtra
The largest rock-cut chaitya hall in India — a barrel-vaulted cave church with a lantern roof carved in stone to mimic wood beams it never had.
Buddha Worshipped by the Gods Indra and Brahma
by Pakistan
Gandhara · South Asian · -1–101 · Schist

0 – 500 CE

Bhimbetka Rock Shelters
Mesolithic onward · 30,000 BCE – historical · India
Painted caves with some of the oldest hunting scenes on earth — a human riding a gigantic red animal, drawn in ochre and charcoal.
Amaravati Stupa Reliefs
Satavahana · 2nd c. BCE – 3rd c. CE · Andhra Pradesh
A Buddhist stupa whose limestone casing was carved with some of the earliest Buddha figures in India. Most pieces are now in the British Museum and the Chennai Government Museum.

Head of the Buddha
Sri Lanka · 6th century-7th century
Anuradhapura- The Abhayagiri Dágoba. View from the west. This Dágoba was built by King Walagam Báhu in the first century before Christ. It is an enormous mass of solid brickwork, 401 feet in diameter at the base, and, although originally much higher, is at the present time about 236 feet in height.

Mihintale- The Etwiháre Dágoba. This Dágoba was constructed by King Bhátya Tissa in the first year of the Christian era, and enshrines within a mass of solid brickwork, nearly 100 feet high, a single hair from the forehead of Buddha.

India, Mathura, Kushan period · c. 142/145+ · gold
Mathura Bodhisattva
Kushan · 1st–3rd c. CE · Uttar Pradesh
Red sandstone figures in a distinctly Indian idiom — broad-shouldered, loose-robed, flatter and more frontal than the Greco-Buddhist Gandhara school evolving to the northwest.
Mathura Railing Pillar
Kushan · 2nd c. CE · India
A red sandstone pillar from a stupa railing — yakshis (tree-spirits) at their erotic fullest, drinkers and musicians around them. Mathura sculpture is carved to be walked around; every side shows someone.
Standing Buddha

Winged Atlas-Hercules
Gandhara · 2nd–3rd c. CE · Pakistan
A schist Hercules with wings, kneeling to support a ceiling — Greek myth holding up a Buddhist stupa. The Gandhara hybrid, born of Alexander's successors and the Kushans.

by Afghanistan or Pakistan
Gandhara · South Asian · 201–500 · Stucco with traces of pigment
Gandhara Buddha
Greco-Buddhist · 1st–5th c. CE · Pakistan/Afghanistan
The first sculptural images of the Buddha, made in schist and draped in togas. A crossroads style born from Alexander's successors.
Mathura Crowned Bodhisattva
Kushan · 3rd c. CE · Mathura
A red sandstone bodhisattva with a jeweled crown and necklace, the body slightly sway-hipped. Mathura's was the first Indian art to give the Buddha a humanoid form.
Gandhara Reliquary Box
Late Kushan · 2nd–4th c. CE · Swat Valley
A bronze reliquary found with Buddhist bone relics inside — small portable architecture, in the shape of a tiny cylindrical stupa, hidden in the heart of a larger one.
Adoring Attendant from a Buddhist Shrine
Afghanistan, Gandhara, Kushan · c. 300s–400s CE · stucco
Naga (Serpent Divinity)

Coin with Figure of an Archer (obverse)
India, Chandragupta II, Gupta Period, late 4th-early 5th century · c. 400 CE · gold
Udayagiri Varaha Relief
Gupta · c. 400 CE · India
A colossal rock-cut panel of Varaha, Vishnu as a man-boar, lifting the earth goddess from the cosmic sea. One of the earliest great Hindu narrative reliefs — imperial propaganda as myth.
Fragment of Head of Buddha
India, Sarnath, Gupta Period (320-647) · 400s CE · sandstone
Head of Vishnu
Northern India, Uttar Pradesh, Mathura · 400s CE · sandstone
Painted Bowl with Fish and Lotus Design

by Pakistan

Female Head
by Pakistan or India
Gandhara · South Asian · 401–700 · Terracotta with traces of red slip
Sarnath Gupta Buddha
Gupta · 5th c. CE
Seated sandstone Buddha preaching the first sermon, hands in dharmachakra mudra, a halo with delicate carving behind his head. The Gupta "Sarnath style" became the template for every Buddha to follow — from Southeast Asia to Japan.
Sigiriya
Anuradhapura period · 477 CE · Sri Lanka
A palace on top of a 660-foot granite plug. Its western face once carried 500 paintings of celestial women; twenty-one still survive.

500 – 1000

Dhamek Stupa
Gupta era · 500 CE · Sarnath
Solid brick stupa marking the spot of the Buddha's first sermon. Its upper band of geometric floral carving is some of the finest Gupta ornament anywhere.
Dashavatara Temple, Deogarh
Gupta · c. 500 CE · Uttar Pradesh
One of the earliest surviving Hindu stone temples, a compact cube topped by a now-damaged tower. Its relief panels — Vishnu asleep on Ananta, Nara-Narayana, the rescue of the elephant king Gajendra — are foundational images of Hindu narrative art.
Swayambhunath
Newar · c. 500 CE · Kathmandu
The 'monkey temple' atop a hill in the Kathmandu Valley, reached by 365 steps flanked by polychrome Buddhas. A 2015 earthquake split several subsidiary shrines; the main stupa, its painted eyes impassive, rode out the shaking.
Bagh Caves
Buddhist · 5th–7th c. CE · Madhya Pradesh
A row of rock-cut monasteries near the Narmada, once painted throughout — a sibling to Ajanta. The frescoes were removed to the Gwalior Museum in 1982 to preserve them.
Mother Goddess
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Udaipur District · c. 600 · schist
Buddhas of Bamiyan
Greco-Buddhist · 6th c. · Afghanistan
Two standing Buddhas, 38 and 55 meters tall, carved into a sandstone cliff on the Silk Road. The Taliban dynamited them in 2001; projection mapping now fills the empty niches on quiet nights.
River Goddess Ganga
Northern India, Uttar Pradesh, Mathura · c. 700 · sandstone
Elephanta Caves
Kalachuri / Chalukya · 5th–8th c. · Mumbai
Rock-cut Shiva temple on an island. The Trimurti — Shiva as creator, preserver, and destroyer — emerges twenty feet tall from the living stone.
One-Faced Linga (Ekamukhalinga)
Eastern India, Bihar, Medieval period, Pala dynasty · 600s–700s · chloritic schist
Devi
Nepal · 600s–700s · gilt bronze
Monolithic Pillar
Northern India, Mathura, early medieval period, 7th-8th century · 600s–700s · red sandstone
Jokhang Temple
Tibetan Buddhist · 642 CE · Lhasa
Tibet's holiest temple, founded by King Songtsen Gampo to house the Jowo Shakyamuni statue brought by his Tang Chinese wife. Pilgrims complete three prostrations every few steps around the Barkhor circuit outside; the flagstones are polished to a mirror.
Pancha Rathas
Pallava · 7th c. · Mahabalipuram
Five monolithic shrines carved from a single granite ridge in the shape of temple chariots. Each is a different architectural experiment — the prototypes for a millennium of South Indian temples.
Descent of the Ganges
Pallava · 7th c. CE · Mahabalipuram
A 27-meter granite relief — elephants, ascetics, gods, gandharvas — with a natural fissure down the middle flooded during rains to become the sacred river itself. The largest open-air relief of the ancient world.
Personification of the Discus
Northeastern India, Bihar, Aphsad, early Pala period, 7th Century · 670 · chlorite
Seated Maitreya
Nepal, late Gupta style, late 7th - early 8th century · late 600s–early 700s · bronze
Vase
Central Asia (Sogdiana) or Tibet · c. late 600s–early 700s · silver with gilded foil
Silver Vessels
Central Asia (Sogdiana) or Tibet · c. late 600s–early 700s · Silver with gilded foil
Rhyton
Central Asia (Sogdiana) or Tibet · c. late 600s–early 700s · silver with gilded foil
Pattadakal
Chalukya dynasty · 7th–8th c. · Karnataka
A proving ground for Indian temple architecture — northern and southern styles built side by side for direct comparison before being exported across the subcontinent.
Vajrapani
India, Kashmir · 700s · bronze with silver overlay and gum tempera
Padmapani Lokeshvara Seated on the Rock
India, Kashmir · 700s · bronze with silver overlay
Buddha Calling the Earth to Witness (Bhumisparshamudra)
by India
Eastern India · South Asian · 701–1000 · Bronze
Shore Temple of Mahabalipuram
Pallava · c. 725 CE · Tamil Nadu
One of the earliest stone temples in South India. It stands on the Bay of Bengal; the 2004 tsunami briefly receded far enough to reveal more submerged temples.
Martand Sun Temple
Karkota Kashmir · 8th c. CE
An early Kashmiri Sun temple on a plateau above the Vale of Kashmir — columns of grey limestone, roof long gone. Destroyed by Sultan Sikandar in the 15th century; the ruins are still astonishing.
Kashmiri Ivory Temptation
Kashmiri Buddhist · 8th c.
A carved ivory panel showing the Buddha meditating while Mara's army hurls weapons that turn to flowers. Kashmiri ivories traveled across the Himalayan trade routes — this one made its way to Tibet.
Kailasa Temple, Ellora
Rashtrakuta · c. 760 CE · Maharashtra
The largest monolithic sculpture on earth — a full Dravida-style temple carved top-down from a single basalt cliff, freeing perhaps two hundred thousand tonnes of rock. Krishna I's masons worked to a plan no draft of which survives; many believe none existed.
Samye Monastery
Tibetan Buddhist · 779 CE · U-Tsang
The first monastery built in Tibet, laid out as a three-dimensional mandala with the central temple representing Mount Meru and four chapels for the cosmic continents. Its founder Trisong Detsen imported Indian masters to ordain the first seven Tibetan monks.
Nalanda Mahavihara
Pala dynasty · 5th–12th c. · Bihar
A Buddhist university that drew ten thousand monks from across Asia. Burned by Bakhtiyar Khalji around 1193; the library, they say, smoked for months.
Birth of the Buddha
Northeastern India, Bengal, Pala dynasty (730-1197) · 800s · Black chlorite
Seated Buddha
India, Bengal(?), Pala period, 9th century · 800s · bronze
Vishnu
Nepal · 800s · schist
God Brahma
by India
Bihar · South Asian · 800–900 · Basalt
Yama as Dharma, the Judge of the Deceased with His Consorts (miniature stele)
Northwest India or Kashmir, 9th century · 800s · schist
Chand Baori
Gurjara-Pratihara · 9th c. · Rajasthan
A thirteen-story stepwell carved with 3,500 narrow steps into dizzying geometric patterns down its walls. One of the oldest and deepest stepwells in India.
Pala Earth-Witnessing Buddha
Pala · 9th c. · Bihar
A black schist Buddha touching the earth to witness his enlightenment, flanked by bodhisattvas. Pala sculpture traveled with pilgrims to Tibet, Nepal, and Burma — and re-seeded the form.
Akshobhya: the Buddha of the East
Northeast India, Bihar, Kurkihar, Pala period · late 800s · bronze with silver and copper inlay
Kali
South India, Tamil Nadu, Chola period (900-13th century) · 900s–1000s · bronze
Stele with Padmapani
India, Nalanda, Pala Period, 10th Century · 900s · black chlorite
Bodhisattva Vajraraksha
Western Tibet · c. 900s · silver
Vak, an Emanation of the Buddha Amitabha
Nepal · 900s · bronze
Goddess Hariti Seated Holding a Child
by India
Bihar · South Asian · 901–1100 · Black stone
God Vishnu with Lakshmi and Sarasvati
by India
Bengal · South Asian · 901–1100 · Bronze
Eight Great Events from the Life of the Buddha
by India
Bihar · South Asian · 901–1000 · Phyllite
Stele with Episode from the Life of the Buddha (Monkey Offers Honey to the Buddha)
by India
Bihar · South Asian · 901–1000 · Black chlorite
Goddess Holding a Lotus
South India, Tamil Nadu, Chola period (900-13th Century) · c. 950 · bronze
Shiva and Parvati
South India, Tamil Nadu, Chola period (900–1200s)) · c. 950–60 · bronze
Lakshmana Temple, Khajuraho
Chandela · 954 CE · Madhya Pradesh
The earliest of Khajuraho's western group — tower-peaks ascending like the Himalayas, and bands of exterior relief that have made the site world-famous for reasons other than ascetic.
Balarama
Bihar · about 958 to 964
Female Tree Deity with Attendant
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Sikar, Harshagiri, 10th century · c. 973 · sandstone
Lovers (Mithuna)
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Sikar, Harshagiri, Pratihara Dynasty, 10th Century · c. 973 · sandstonewith limestone wash
Brahma-Shiva
South India, Tamil Nadu, Chola dynasty, late 10th - early 11th century · late 900s–1000s · granite
Gommateshwara Statue at Shravanabelagola
by Aristanemi
Ganga dynasty · 983 CE · Karnataka
A 17-meter monolithic nude of the Jain ascetic Bahubali, absorbed in meditation so deep that vines have grown up his legs. Every twelve years, pilgrims anoint the figure with milk, turmeric, and sandalwood in the Mahamastakabhisheka festival.
Monkey General Hanuman
Southern India, Chola period (900s–1200s) · c. 1000 · bronze
Shiva as Lord of Music
South India, Tamil Nadu, Chola period (900-13th century) · c. 1000 · bronze
Buddha Giving the First Sermon (Dharmachakrapravartanamudra)
by India
Bihar · South Asian · 995–1005 · Black stone
Tabo Monastery Murals
Tibetan Buddhist · 996 CE onward · Spiti, India
A mud-walled monastery in a cold desert valley, its dim interior packed with painted mandalas and stucco bodhisattvas. Perhaps the oldest continuously functioning Buddhist monastery in the Himalayas.

1000 – 1500

Nataraja (Shiva as Lord of Dance)
Chola dynasty · 10th–11th c. · Tamil Nadu
Shiva dances the universe into being and out of it again, surrounded by a ring of flame, trampling the demon of ignorance.
Khajuraho Temples
Chandela dynasty · 950–1050 · Madhya Pradesh
Hindu and Jain temples covered in dense sculpture — gods, battles, and the frank erotic scenes the site is best known for.
Chola Bronzes
Chola dynasty · 9th–13th century · Tamil Nadu
Hollow-cast bronze icons of Shiva, Vishnu, and their consorts, produced by the lost-wax method for temple processions. The dynasty's patronage pushed Indian metal sculpture to its aesthetic high point — even today casters in Swamimalai work to thousand-year-old Chola templates.
Nalanda Earth-Witnessing Buddha
Pala · late 10th–11th c. · Nalanda
A black schist stele — Buddha seated in bhumisparsha mudra, Avalokiteshvara and Maitreya standing beside him. The Pala style that defined Himalayan Buddhist art for centuries.
Eleven-Headed Avalokiteshvara
Western Himalayan · c. 1000 · Kashmir
A brass Avalokiteshvara with eleven heads stacked in three tiers — each head watches a different direction, seeing the suffering of all beings. Silver and copper inlay for eyes, lips, markings.
Nataraja, Shiva as the Lord of Dance
South India, Tamil Nadu, Chola period (900-1200s) · 1000s · Bronze
Female torso
Northeastern India, Pala dynasty (730-1197) · 1000s · black chlorite
Vishnu Attended by Chakrapurusa and Shankhapurusa
East India, Bengal, Pala period, 11th-12th century · 1000s–1100s · bronze with silver inlay
Goddess Uma
Nepal · 1000s · gilt bronze and semiprecious stones
Seven Leaves from a Manuscript of the Gandavyuha-sutra (Scripture of the Supreme Array)
Nepal · 1000s-1100s · Gum tempera and ink on palm leaves
Avalokitesvara Padmapani: Bodhisattva of Mercy Bearing a Lotus
Nepal · c. 1000s · bronze with gilding, pigment, and semiprecious stones
Goddess Siddhalakshmi
India, Kashmir · 1000s · bronze
Miniature Votive Stupa
Eastern India, Bihar · c. 1000s · bronze with inlay of semiprecious stones
Votive initiation card (tsakali) with preaching Buddha Shakyamuni
Western Tibet, Tholing Monastery · 1000s · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Shiva as Slayer of the Elephant Demon
South India, Tamil Nadu, early Chola Period, 11th century · 1000s · granite
Bodhisattva Simhanada Lokeshvara
by India
Bihar · South Asian · 1001–1100 · Basalt
Brihadeeswarar Temple
Chola dynasty · 1010 · Thanjavur
A granite tower 216 feet high, capped with a single 80-ton stone. A thousand years old and still in daily ritual use.
Gangaikonda Cholapuram
Chola · 1025 · Tamil Nadu
Rajendra Chola built this temple to commemorate his invasion of the Gangetic plain, bringing back a pot of Ganges water poured into the sanctum. The vimana is a softer, more feminine echo of his father's Brihadeeswarar at Thanjavur.
Modhera Sun Temple
Chaulukya dynasty · 1026 · Gujarat
Temple to Surya oriented so the first rays of the equinox sun strike the inner sanctum. A cistern called Ramakunda in front holds over a hundred miniature shrines along its steps.
Goddess Vajravarahi
Nepal · c. 1040–c. 1310 · unbaked clay and pigment
Hanuman Conversing
Chola · 11th c. · Tamil Nadu
A bronze Hanuman, monkey general of the Ramayana, standing in animated mid-speech — hand raised, tail curled up. Chola temple bronzes were paraded in festivals; weight and lost-wax craft for a god who moved.
Trident with Shiva as Half-Woman (Ardhanarishvara)
South India, Tamil Nadu, Chola period (900-13th Century) · c. 1050 · bronze
Bodhisattva of Compassion as Lokeshvara the Sky Flyer (Khasarpana)
Eastern India, Bihar, Pala dynasty, reign of Ramapala (reign 1078/79–at least 1130) · late 1000s · Kaolin with traces of pigment
Ganesha
South India, Medieval period, Chola dynasty(10th-13th century) · c. 1070 · bronze
Rani ki Vav
Chaulukya dynasty · 11th c. · Gujarat
A subterranean stepwell dug by Queen Udayamati for her husband. Seven levels down, 500 sculptures of Vishnu avatars and apsaras. Silted over by the Saraswati; rediscovered in the 1980s.
Altarpiece with the First Jaina Tirthankara Rishabhanatha Surrounded by Twenty-Three Other Tirthankaras
by India
India · South Asian · 1089 · Brass inlaid with copper and silver
Shadakshari Mahavidya
by India
Bihar · 1099–1199 · Gilt bronze with silver and copper inlay
Alchi Monastery
Tibetan Buddhist · 11th–13th c. · Ladakh
Six temples on the Indus riverbank, their interiors covered in Kashmir-style murals unlike anywhere else in Tibet — sensuous, courtly, entirely different from the iconic Buddhism of later centuries.
Bodhisattva Maitreya: The Future Buddha
East India, Bengal, Pala period, 12th century · 1100s · silver inlaid with turquoise, copper, brass and gold; gilt bronze lotus
Standing Manjusri (Arapacana Form)
India, Pala, 12th-13th century · 1100s-1200s · bronze
Seated Tara
India, Bengal, late Pala dynasty, 12th Century · 1100s · bronze
Guhyasamaja Manjuvajra, an Esoteric Form of Bodhisattva Manjushri
by India
Bihar · Himalayan · 1100–1199 · Brass with silver
Shiva Saint: Manikkavachakar
South India, Tamil Nadu, 12th-13th century · 1100s–1200s · bronze
Chennakeshava Temple, Belur
Hoysala · 1117 · Karnataka
A Hoysala masterwork covered in a continuous band of relief — madanikas, elephants, musicians — carved from soapstone so soft the sculptors could almost draw on it.
Folio 1 (recto), from a Manuscript of the Perfection of Wisdom in Eight Thousand Lines (Ashtasahasrika Prajnaparamita-sutra)
Eastern India, Bihar, Vikramashila Monastery. Paper: Nepal, Kathmandu · Sunday, September 14, 1119 (year 239 of the Newar Samvat in the month of Ashvina) · Palm leaf backed with yellow paper

Eastern India, Bihar, Vikramashila Monastery. Painting: Nepal, Kathmandu · Sunday, September 14, 1119 (year 239 of the Newar Samvat in the month of Ashvina) · Gum tempera and ink on palm leaf
Dilwara Temples
Jain · 11th–13th c. · Mount Abu
White marble carved so finely the columns look more like lace than stone. Every surface is covered — floor, ceiling, arch.
Gal Vihara
Polonnaruwa · 12th c. · Sri Lanka
Four colossal Buddhas carved from a single granite wall — seated, standing, reclining, meditating. The face of the reclining Buddha is nine meters long.
Jagannath Temple, Puri
Eastern Ganga · 12th c. · Odisha
Home of the annual Rath Yatra — giant wooden chariots hauled through the streets carrying the three deities. The temple's cooks feed the god daily from one of the largest kitchens in the world.
Hoysaleswara Temple
Hoysala · 1150 · Halebidu, Karnataka
Carved from chloritic schist, which hardens after quarrying, enabling the Hoysala sculptors to cut almost impossibly thin details. Elephants, musicians, and gods wrap the plinth in horizontal registers that add up to miles of relief never repeating itself.
Tantric Buddha Vairochana as Vajrasattva
Central Tibet · c. 1150–1200 · gum tempera, ink, and gold on cloth
One of Three Leaves from the Perfection of Wisdom Sutra (Ashtasahasrika Prajnaparamitasutra)
by India or Bangladesh
India · 1155–1185 · Opaque watercolor aand ink on palm leaf
Airavatesvara Temple
Chola · 1166 · Darasuram, Tamil Nadu
Rajaraja II's jewel-box Shiva temple, its front mandapa shaped like a chariot pulled by stone horses. The plinth is carved with musicians, dancers, and — charmingly — optical-illusion animals: a bull that doubles as an elephant depending on which end you start from.
Figure of a Maitreya on a Tiered Pedestal
Nepal · c. 1200 · gilded wood with polychrome
Shiva
South India, Chola period (900–1200s) · 1200s–1300s · bronze

Central Tibet · 1200s · gilt bronze, inlaid with gold, silver, copper, turquoise, lapis and coral
Painted Banner (Thangka) with the Medicine Buddha (Bhaishajyaguru)
by Central Tibet
Tibet · Himalayan · 1201–1400 · Pigment and gold on cotton

by India
Karnataka · South Asian · 1201–1300 · Chlorite phyllite
Portraits of Two Lineage Masters of the Kagyu Order: Phagmo Drupa (1110–1170) and Tashipel (1142–1210)
Central Tibet · c. 1236–1310 · Gum tempera on sized cotton
Konark Sun Temple
Eastern Ganga dynasty · 13th c. · Odisha
A massive stone chariot for the sun god, pulled by seven horses, wheels carved to tell the time from their shadows.
Yoga Narashimha, Vishnu in his Man-Lion Avatar
South India, late Chola Period (900-13th Century) · c. 1250 · bronze
Jain monk with disciple and two laymen, two nuns, and a laywoman, folio 22 (recto) from a Yoga-shastra of Hemachandra
Western India, Gujarat · c. 1275 · Gum tempera and ink on palm leaf
Folio 22 from a Yoga-shastra of Hemachandra: Jain Monk with disciple and two laymen, two Nuns, and a laywoman (recto); Text (verso)
Western India, Gujarat, 13th century · c. 1275 · Gum tempera and ink on palm leaf
Green Tara (Aniko)
by Aniko
Central Tibet · Aniko · c. 1260
Attributed to Aniko, the Nepali prodigy who led the Yuan court's Buddhist ateliers. Green Tara extends her right foot, one hand offering the varada mudra — the compassionate bodhisattva of swift response.
Chennakeshava Temple, Somanathapura
Hoysala · 1268 · Karnataka
A star-plan temple with three shrines on a single platform, surrounded by a cloistered courtyard. Every surface is carved: gods in scalloped niches, musicians in frothing bands, a frieze of marching elephants at ground level supporting the whole weight of the building.
Folio 2 from a Parshvanatha Charitra (Life and Stories of Lord Parshva) of Bhavadeva-suri: Monk preaching to a disciple with a lay audience (recto); Text (verso)

by Choying Dorje, the Tenth Black Hat Karmapa
China and Tibet, Embroidery: China, Yuan dynasty (1279–1368); painting: Tibet · painting c. 1650; top and bottom borders c. 1350 · Painting: gum tempera, gold, and silver on silk; lateral borders: silk damask with needleloop embroidery; top and bottom panels: embroidered silk, lead, and silver paper; curtain: silk tabby; dowels: wood, rawhide, and leather
Lankathilaka Vihara
Gampola · 14th c. · Sri Lanka
A small Buddhist temple on a rock, its whitewashed walls still holding murals. Partly Hindu in plan, partly Buddhist in image — a Sri Lankan hybrid of both traditions.
Twenty-three Deity Nairatma Mandala
Central Tibet, Sakya-affiliated monastery, 14th century · c. 1375 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on cloth
Birth of Mahavira, folio 40 (verso) from a Kalpa-sutra

Painted Banner (Thangka) of Vajrasattva Mandala
by Tibet
Tibet · Himalayan · 1399–1499 · Pigment on cloth
Thangka Painting
Tibetan Buddhist · c. 11th century onward · Himalayan region
Scroll paintings on cotton or silk, bordered with brocade, depicting deities in complex mandalic geometries for visualization practice. Pigments are ground from powdered turquoise, cinnabar, and malachite — gold highlights are burnished with a dog's tooth.
Bodhisattva of Wisdom (Manjushri)

by Tibet
Tibet · Himalayan · 1401–1500 · Opaque watercolor with gold on cotton
Ritual Flaying Knife
Sino-Tibetan · c. 1410 · Derge
A tantric ritual knife (kartika) from the early Ming court — used symbolically to flay the ego, not any body. Gilt copper, with Yama's attributes on the handle.
Gyantse Kumbum
Tibetan Buddhist · 1427 · Tibet
A nine-storey stupa with 75 chapels spiralling up it — each chapel a different deity in a full pantheon. A three-dimensional mandala you walk through.
Folio 2, from a Great Poem about Twos (Dvyashraya Mahakavya) of Hemachandra with Commentary by Abhayatilaka

Western India, Gujarat · c. 1450–75 · Gum tempera and gold on paper; 18th century gold flecked paper border
Hampi (Vijayanagara)
Vijayanagara Empire · 14th–16th c. · Karnataka
Capital of the last great Hindu empire in the south. Sacked in 1565; the boulder-strewn ruins sprawl over 16 square miles.
Ranakpur Jain Temple
Jain · 15th c. · Rajasthan
1,444 carved marble columns, no two alike. The complex is aligned so that the sun reaches the central sanctum for only a few days a year.
Seated Jina Rishabha Enshrined, from a Jain Manuscript: Kalpa-Sutra

Western India, Gujarat · c. 1475–1500 · gum tempera and gold on paper
Mehrangarh Fort
Rathore dynasty · 1459 onward · Jodhpur
A hundred-meter cliff-top fort in the Blue City of Jodhpur. Its walls still bear handprints of the widows of maharajas who threw themselves on the pyre in the medieval practice of sati.

Tibet · c. 1500 · ink and watercolor on cotton
Iconographic Drawings: Vaishravana, Yama, Ushnishavijaya, Chunda (?), and Buddha (recto)
Tibet · c. 1500 · ink and watercolor on cotton
Eleven-Headed, Thousand-Armed Bodhisattva of Compassion (Avalokiteshvara)
Western Tibet · c. 1500 · Gum tempera and ink on cotton
Altarpiece with Twenty-Second Jaina Tirthankara Neminatha Surrounded by Twenty-Three Other Tirthankaras
by India
India · South Asian · 1498 · Brass inlaid with silver

1500 – 1800

Boudhanath Stupa
Newar/Tibetan Buddhist · 15th century · Kathmandu
One of the largest stupas in Asia, thirty-six meters across, its dome topped by the painted eyes of the Buddha watching in four directions. Circumambulated daily by Tibetan refugees, pilgrims, and locals — prayer-wheel grooves are worn smooth into the granite.
Thangka of Green Tara
Tibetan Buddhist · 13th c. onward
The green-skinned female bodhisattva of swift compassion, one foot extended to step into action. Thangkas were painted for meditation — a portable cosmos on cloth.
Durga Slaying the Demon Buffalo
Nepal · 1500s · bronze
Achala
Nepal · 1500s · brass or bronze; turquoise
Painted Banner (Thangka) of Bon Deity Tonpa Shenrap
by Tibet
Tibet · 1500–1599 · Opaque watercolor with gold on cotton
Painted Banner (Thangka) of Bodhisattva Maitreya Surrounded by his Retinue
by Tibet
Tibet · Himalayan · 1501–1600 · Opaque watercolor and gold on cotton
Oil Lamp with Christian Crosses
by Sri Lanka
Sri Lanka · South Asian · 1501–1700 · Bronze
Buddha Triumphing over Mara (Maravijaya)
by Tibet
Tibet · Himalayan · 1501–1600 · Gilt bronze
Personified Ritual Dagger (Vajrakila) in Ritual Embrace (Yab-yum)
by Tibet
Tibet · Himalayan · 1501–1600 · Copper repoussé over wood
Virupaksha Temple, Hampi
Vijayanagara · 7th century onward · Karnataka
The only temple in Hampi still in continuous worship after the sack of 1565. Its nine-tiered gopura pours down layers of painted deities; in the courtyard a pinhole in the outer wall projects the gopura inverted onto the opposite wall — a Vijayanagara camera obscura.
Vitthala Temple Stone Chariot
Vijayanagara · 16th c. · Hampi
A stone chariot with stone wheels that are said to have actually rotated. Built as the shrine for Vitthala's vahana, Garuda, in the empire's last great capital.
Page from the Panj Ganj (Five Treasures) of Abd al-Rahman Jami (Persian, 1414–1492), with two Persian masnavis (narrative poems): Yusuf va Zulaykha (Joseph and Zulaykha) and Subhat al-abrar (Rosary of the Righteous)
by Mushfiq
Northern India, Mughal, early 17th century · Text c. 1500–1520; painting, borders, and illuminations added 1603–7 Text copied by Sultan-Ali Mashhadi (Persian, active early 1500s) for Badi al-Zaman Mirza, ruler of Herat Painted insets and borders by Mushfiq (Indian, active 1580s–early 1600s) Text copied in Herat Painting, borders, and illuminations made in the Deccan for Mughal minister Abd al-Rahim the Khan Khanan · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Page from a Panj Ganj (Five Treasures) of Abd al-Rahman Jami (Persian, 1414–1492), with two Persian masnavis: Yusuf va Zulaykha (Joseph and Zulaykha) and Subhat al-abrar (Rosary of the Righteous) (verso)
by Mushfiq
Northern India, Mughal, early 17th century · 1520–1607 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Folio B: Folio from the "Five Treasures" (Panj Ganj) of Jami (recto)
by Mushfiq
Northern India, Mughal, early 17th century · 1520–1607 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Lepakshi Temple
Vijayanagara · 16th c. · Andhra Pradesh
A granite temple to Shiva whose ceiling holds the largest surviving Vijayanagara mural. One of its columns hangs a fraction of an inch above the floor — pilgrims pass cloth beneath it.
Lotus Mahal
Vijayanagara · 16th c. · Hampi
A pavilion in the zenana quarter with lotus-shaped scalloped arches. Architectural hybrid: Hindu temple superstructure atop Islamic multifoil arches — courtly syncretism in stone.
Torana with Garuda attacking a Naga and a Nagini
Nepal · 1550-1600
The Parrot Addresses Khujasta at the Beginning of the Seventh Night, from a Tuti-nama (Tales of a Parrot)
Mughal India, court of Akbar (reigned 1556–1605) · c. 1560 · gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
The Raja’s son vows to sever his head and offer it to the image if he is united with the princess he has seen in the temple, from a Tuti-nama (Tales of a Parrot): Thirty-fourth Night
Mughal India, court of Akbar (reigned 1556–1605) · c. 1560 · gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Humayun's Tomb
Mughal · 1570 · Delhi
The first garden tomb of the Mughals and the prototype for the Taj Mahal eighty years later. A red sandstone dome in a four-quartered paradise garden.
Fatehpur Sikri
Mughal · Akbar · 1571 · Uttar Pradesh
A red sandstone capital built and abandoned in fifteen years when the water ran out. The ghost city of the greatest of the Mughals.
Krishna Lifts Mount Govardhan
Mughal · c. 1590
Krishna holds the mountain over his village like an umbrella against Indra's deluge. A Mughal-style Harivamsa, commissioned by Akbar — Hindu myth painted in the royal Islamic atelier.
Deccan Prince on Composite Elephant
Deccan · Golconda · c. 1590
A courtly prince rides an elephant whose body is a tangle of smaller animals — the composite beast trope in Islamic painting. Golconda, not Mughal, shows a distinctive Persianate coloration.
Charminar
Qutb Shahi · 1591 · Hyderabad
Four monumental minarets at the heart of old Hyderabad, crossing four broad avenues at their base — an arch-plus-minaret quartet supposedly built to mark the end of a plague.
Meenakshi Temple
Nayak period · 16th–17th c. · Madurai
Fourteen gateway towers covered in thousands of painted figures. The whole complex is repainted every twelve years.
Jain Ascetic (Basavana)
by Basavana
Mughal · Basavana · c. 1600
A Mughal court painter's study of a naked Digambara Jain ascetic — Emperor Akbar's interest in all religions meant his ateliers painted sages of every tradition in fine-line detail.
Pair of Deity Earrings with Vishnu on Garuda (front) and chepu (monster mask) (back)
Nepal, Kathmandu Valley · 1600s or 1700s · Gold set with precious and semi-precious stones
Pendant with Four-armed Green Vishnu on a lotus with Nagas
Nepal, Kathmandu Valley · 1600s or 1700s · Gold set with precious and semiprecious stones
Earring with Four-Armed Vishnu Riding Garuda with Nagas (serpent divinities)
Nepal, Kathmandu Valley · 1600s or 1700s · repousse gold with pearls
Pair of Earrings with Four-Armed Vishnu Riding Garuda with Nagas (serpent divinities)

Falcon on a Perch

Vajrapani Embroidered Mount with Garuda

India · South Asian · 1601–1800 · Brass with pierced and engraved decoration
Portrait of a Beauty
by India
India · mughal · 1601–1700 · Opaque watercolor and gold on paper
Zulaykha in her palace and as an old woman with Joseph, from a Panj Ganj (Five Treasures) of Abd al-Rahman Jami (Persian, 1414–1492); verso from a Panj Ganj of Abd al-Rahman Jami (Persian, 1414–1492), with two masnavis: Yusuf va Zulaykha (Joseph and Zulaykha) and Khirad-nama-i Iskandari (Alexander’s Book of Wisdom)
by Mushfiq
Western Afghanistan, Herat (text); Mughal India (paintings) · Text copied by Sultan-Ali Mashhadi (Persian, 1453–1520) for Badi al-Zaman Mirza of Herat Painting, borders, and illumination by Mushfiq (Indian, active 1580s–early 1600s); Text copied in Herat; Painting, borders, and illuminations made in the Deccan for Mughal minister Abd al-Rahim the Khan Khanan · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
The Court of Ravana
Mughal Ramayana · c. 1605
A crowded throne room of the demon king in Lanka — demon attendants, musicians, intrigue. From a manuscript commissioned by a Mughal noble to translate Valmiki's Sanskrit into Persian-style painting.
Portrait of Maharaja Rai Singh of Bikaner (reigned 1574–1612)
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Bikaner · c. 1610 · Gum tempera and gold on paper
Akbar's Tomb
Mughal · 1605–1613 · Sikandra, Agra
A five-story mausoleum on a platform in a paradise garden. Akbar designed much of it himself; Jahangir's later changes produced the strange tiered result.
A Young, Angry Heroine, Folio 28 from a Rasamanjari
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Mewar · c. 1615–20 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Rasikapriya: Abhisarika Nayika
Rajasthani · c. 1615 · Northwestern India
A heroine (abhisarika) braves the dark and the snakes to meet her lover — defined in the Rasikapriya as one of eight classical love-types. Rajput painters painted every one.
From Dohras (Songs) 40 and 42 from the Kitab-i Nauras (Book of Nine Essences) of Sultan Ibrahim Adil Shah II of Bijapur (r. 1580–1627); verso: From Dohras (Songs) 40 and 36 from the Kitab-i Nauras of Sultan Ibrahim Adil Shah II
by Khalilullah Butshikan
India, Bijapur, Deccan · 1618 · ink with two-tone gold on paper, double-sided
Portrait of Raja Jagat Singh of Nurpur (reigned 1618–46)
by Bichitr
Mughal India, Court of Jahangir (reigned 1605 - 27), possibly Lahore · probably 1619 · Gum tempera and gold on paper
Jahangir in Darbar
Mughal miniature · c. 1620
A crowded court of the Mughal emperor in audience — every noble identified by a tiny inscription, each face individual. The Jahangir-nama was a painted autobiography.
Shah Jahan on a Terrace
by Chitarman
Mughal · Chitarman · 1628
The Mughal emperor stands in profile on a palace terrace, holding a pendant set with his own portrait. Painted the year he succeeded to the throne — political self-presentation at imperial scale.
Portrait of Murad Bakhsh (1624–61)
by Balchand
Mughal India, court of Shah Jahan (reigned 1628–58) · c. 1635; borders c. 1700s · gum tempera and gold on paper
An African Lyre Player (recto)
India, Deccan, 17th century · c. 1640–60 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Girls Spraying Each Other at Holi
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Bikaner · c. 1640–50 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Temple Banner with pilgrimage sites and scenes from the "Svayambhu-purana" (Ancient Text of the Primordial Buddha)
Nepal, Kathmandu Valley · 1635 · Gum tempera and gold on cotton
Punakha Dzong
Bhutan · 1637 · Punakha
Bhutan's second dzong, built at the confluence of two rivers and still the winter home of the monastic body. Its five-story central utse leans out over the water; inside, eighteenth-century murals of the Buddha's life glow in whale-oil lamplight.
Taj Mahal
Mughal · 1632–1653 · Agra
A tomb Shah Jahan built for his wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth. The marble is inlaid with 28 kinds of gem and stone.
Rosette of Shah Jahan
Mughal · c. 1645
An illuminated rosette bearing Shah Jahan's titles in Persian — the emperor as "Second Lord of the Conjunction," claimant to Timur's universal empire. Opening page of his imperial album.
Bodhisattva of Compassion (Avalokiteshvara)
by Choying Dorje, the Tenth Black Hat Karmapa
Tibet · 1647–48 or 1658 · ivory
Red Fort
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1648 · Delhi
Red sandstone walls two kilometers around. Inside, the Diwan-i-Khas held the Peacock Throne until Nadir Shah of Persia took it to Isfahan in 1739.
Bejeweled Buddha Preaching
Nepal · 1648 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on cloth
Amber Fort
Rajput · 16th–18th c. · Jaipur
Hilltop fort-palace of the Kachhwaha dynasty. The Hall of Mirrors inside glitters with thousands of convex glass inlays; a single candle lights the whole room.
Mughal Miniature Painting
Mughal · 16th–18th c. · North India
Album leaves commissioned by the Mughal emperors — historical chronicles, portraits, and natural history drawn with jeweller's precision.
Maru Ragini, from a Ragamala (Garland of Melodies)
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Amber or Southwestern India, Deccan · 1650–80 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper
Locket
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Jaipur · 1700s · gold and enamel
Woman Longing for Her Lover: Verati Ragini of Dipak, from a Ragamala
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Kota · 1650–60 · gum tempera, ink, gold, and silver on paper
Gol Gumbaz
Adil Shahi · 1656 · Bijapur
Tomb of Mohammed Adil Shah. The dome is the second-largest in the world, and the whispering gallery underneath it repeats a voice seven times.
Jama Masjid, Delhi
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1656 · Delhi
The largest mosque in India — a red sandstone and marble courtyard for 25,000. Built in the same decade as the Taj Mahal, by the same emperor.
Raja Ram Chand of Amber (r. 1667–88) Hunting Wild Boar
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Amber · c. 1670 · gum tempera and gold on paper
Radha
Basohli · ca. 1660 - ca. 1670

Krishna Fluting
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Bundi · c. 1675–1700 · Gum tempera on paper
Patan Durbar Square
Malla dynasty · 17th century · Lalitpur, Nepal
The royal plaza of the Malla kings of Patan, edged with Newar pagodas and a stone Krishna Mandir carved in imitation of north Indian shikharas. Several temples collapsed in 2015 and have been reconstructed with timber dowels rather than steel.
Prince Shreyamsa dreams of the coming of Rishabha, from a Panchakalyanaka (Five Auspicious Events)

Rasikapriya: Shatha Nayaka
Rajasthani · c. 1680 · Northwestern India
A folio from the Rasikapriya of Keshavdas — Krishna and Radha quarreling. "Shatha Nayaka" is the lover-type who denies his affair. Rajput painting illustrated types of lovers and their moods.

Northern India, Himachal Pradesh, Pahari kingdom of Basohli · c. 1690 · Gum tempera and ink on paper
Paro Taktsang (Tiger's Nest Monastery)
Bhutan · 1692 · Paro
Perched on a cliff 900 meters above the Paro valley, on the ledge where Guru Rinpoche was said to have meditated for three years, three months, three weeks, three days, and three hours. A 1998 fire destroyed most of it; the rebuild faithfully repeats the original timber joinery.

Golden Temple
Sikh · 16th–19th c. · Amritsar
The holiest site of Sikhism. Marble walls inlaid with gemstone, upper floors covered in gold leaf, the whole thing floating on a sacred pool.
Thanjavur Painting
Nayaka / Maratha · 16th–19th c. · Tamil Nadu
Devotional paintings built up in gesso, then layered in gold leaf, semi-precious stones, and mirrored glass. Surface so encrusted the figures seem to float off the board.

Basholi Painting
Pahari miniature · c. 1730
Bulging eyes, hot red backgrounds, jewel-like beetle-wing-case insets for emeralds — the Basholi school is Rajput painting at its most fierce and stylized.
Sultan Ibrahim ibn Adham of Balkh Visited by Angels
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Bundi · c. 1740 · gum tempera and gold on paper
Jantar Mantar
Rajput · Jai Singh II · 1734 · Jaipur
An observatory of huge masonry instruments — sundials ninety feet tall, instruments to locate any star. Built to correct the drifting calendars of Mughal India.

Ravana Battles Jatayu
Kishangarh · 1744 · Rajasthan
A painted folio from a Jain story — the demon king Ravana fights the great vulture Jatayu, defeating him by throwing stones into his mouth. Kishangarh style at its most dramatic and particular.
Folio 18 from a Chandana Malayagiri Varta (Story of King Chandana and Queen Malayagiri) of Karamachand: Ravana battles the great vulture Jatayu and defeats him by throwing stones in his mouth (recto); Jatayu approaches Rama and Lakshmana who are wondering where Sita could be (verso)
Northwestern India, Rajasthan, Rajput Kingdom of Kishangarh · 1744–45 · Gum tempera, ink, and gold on paper

Bani Thani
Kishangarh miniature · c. 1750
The elongated profile of a poetess in the court of Raja Sawant Singh — arched brows, fishlike eye, pursed lip. A regional style turned into Rajasthan's most distinctive visual signature.

A Bhagavata Purana folio — Krishna and Satyabhama besiege the citadel of the demon king Naraka. Bright Newari pigments in a compressed perspective.

Great Indian Fruit Bat
by Bhawani Das
Company School · Bhawani Das · c. 1780
An Indian court painter working for British naturalists delivered this bat — wing fully splayed, every vein inked — with a scientific accuracy European artists couldn't match.
Indra Worships Ganesha
Pahari (Kangra) · c. 1780
A Gita Govinda folio — Indra, king of gods, bowing to the elephant-headed Ganesha. Soft Kangra washes, a rare theme in which the greatest god kneels to a smaller one.

Kangra Painting
Pahari miniature · 18th c. · Himalayan foothills
Cool greens and misty mountains, Krishna and Radha, lovers in garden pavilions. The Kangra school of Rajput painting is the visual corollary of Hindi love poetry.

1800 onward

Jain Manuscript Cover
Gujarati · c. 1800 · Western India
An embroidered silk cover for a palm-leaf Jain manuscript — the inner text a Kalpasutra, the outer cover a protective textile. Jain libraries preserved manuscripts for centuries this way.

Mysore Palace
Indo-Saracenic · 1912 · Karnataka
The current incarnation — fourth to stand on the site — is a wedding cake of Hindu, Mughal, Rajput, and Gothic elements. Lit up nightly by nearly a hundred thousand bulbs.
Victoria Memorial
British Raj · 1906–1921 · Kolkata
A white marble domed memorial to Queen Victoria, built at the height of imperial self-regard. Now a museum of the Raj and the nationalism that ended it.
Somnath Temple
Chaulukya → rebuilt 1951 · Gujarat
A Hindu pilgrimage site destroyed and rebuilt seven times — plundered most famously by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1025. The current temple was built after Indian independence, at the sea's edge.
Rock Garden of Chandigarh
by Nek Chand
Outsider · Nek Chand · 1957 onward · India
A secret sculpture park a road inspector built on the sly from broken bangles, electric switches, ceramic shards, and urban trash. Discovered by authorities in 1975; preserved on public demand.

Lotus Temple
by Fariborz Sahba
Modernist Bahá'í · Fariborz Sahba · 1986 · Delhi
A Bahá'í House of Worship in 27 free-standing white marble petals arranged in three clusters — one of the most visited buildings in the world.
